---
title: "Expert Report for the Royal Commission on Antisemitism and Social Cohesion"
author: "Daniel Allington"
date: "2026-04-18"
output:
  word_document: default
  html_document: default
bibliography: "royal-commission.bib"
documentclass: article
csl: "chicago-fullnote-mod.csl"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)

library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
```

# What is antisemitism?
As the Oxford English Dictionary makes clear, the word 'antisemitism' refers, in its everyday sense, to '[p]rejudice, hostility, or discrimination against Jewish people' [@oed_antisemitism_2025, sense 1]. However, scholars often use the word in a more specific sense, refering not to 'th[e] entire history [of anti-Jewish _praxis_], from its earliest beginnings in classical and pagan times through to the present', but rather to the bigotry and persecution faced by Jews in Europe and its diaspora during a specific historical period which 'beg[an] sometime in the latter part of the nineteenth century' [@seymour_continuity_2019, p. 12]. A discussion of why this is the case may serve to clarify much about the nature of Jew-hate, and also to elucidate key debates over the term itself.

Until the end of the 18th century, Jews were almost universally oppressed on religious grounds. In Europe, full legal rights were available only to Christians, and a range of superstitions arose from the idea that Jews bore historic guilt for the killing of Jesus Christ: they were customarily accused of practising witchcraft, worshiping the devil, and murdering non-Jewish children in order to consume their blood [@trachtenberg_devil_1943]. In the Muslim world, Jews were permitted to reside only as _dhimmis_: second-class subjects of the ruler, unable to rely on the full protection of the law, yet obliged to pay a special tax and to follow often humiliating restrictions designed to reinforce their precarious and inferior status [@lewis_jews_1987, p. 14; @gilbert_ishmaels_2011, p. 87-88]. However, after the Enlightenment, Jews began to achieve emancipation. In 1791, France became the first Christian country to grant full rights to Jews. Over the next hundred years, most European countries followed suit --- except within the Russian Empire, where Jews continued not only to lack rights but also to be persecuted in the most brutal and public fashion --- and, while much of the Muslim world lagged behind in this respect (see in particular the dreadful conditions which prevailed in Persia) [@allington_islamist_2025, p. 26-28], modernising rulers of the Ottoman Empire provided equality for Jews and abolished the _dhimma_ system in a series of reforms beginning in 1839 and spanning several decades.

It was during this period that the myth of a worldwide Jewish conspiracy began. As Norman Cohn explains in the classic study, this myth 'represents a modern adaptation of th[e] ancient demonological tradition' of Christian anti-Judaism, and holds that

> there exists a secret Jewish government which, though a world-wide network of camouflaged agencies and organisations, controls political parties and governments, the press and public opinion, banks and economic developments. The secret government is supposed to be doing this in pursuance of an age-old plan and with the single aim of achieving Jewish dominion over the entire world; and it is also supposed to be perilously near to achieving this aim. [@cohn_warrant_1967, p. 22-23]

France was, as noted above, the first country to give full rights to Jews, and it was in France that the Jewish conspiracy myth appears first to have germinated, expressed in a forged letter purporting to have been written by a Florentine army officer by the name of J.B. Simonini, and addressed in 1806 to l'Abbé Barruel, the author of a conspiracy theory attributing the French Revolution to the machinations of the Freemasons and the Bavarian Illuminati, to which the fraudulent 'Simonini letter' became a sort of postscriptum [@cohn_warrant_1967, p. 27]. The Jews of what is now Germany were emancipated at different points in the 19th century, depending on where they resided, and it was among German writers from the 1860s onwards that the conspiracy myth really took root. 

Hermann Goedsche's 1868 novel _Biarritz_, published under the pseudonym of Sir John Retcliffe, included a chapter in which a group of rabbis plot to complete their fiendish takeover of the world [@cohn_warrant_1967, p. 34-36]. With minor adaptations, this text was later republished as the supposed real words of a leading rabbi [@cohn_warrant_1967, p. 36-37], from which Tsarist agents would later plagiarise much of the content of _The Protocols of the Elders of Zion_: a literary forgery falsely presented as the record of a speech given by Theodor Herzl at the 1897 First Zionist Congress [@cohn_warrant_1967, p. 69]. It was also in Germany that the myth became embedded in an anti-Jewish political programme, in the works of a radical left-wing writer called Wilhelm Marr.

Marr understood the people of Europe to be divided into three 'races', which he dubbed the 'Slavic', the 'Latin', and the 'Germanic' [@zimmermann_wilhelm_1986, p. 66]. This terminology was linguistic in origin, these being the names of the transnational families into which fell the languages spoken by members of these supposed 'races'. For Marr, 'race' was more a matter of culture than of biology, and he referred to the characteristic culture of the 'Germanic race' as _Germanenthum_, generally translated as 'Germanism' or 'Germanicness', which --- with its antiquarian implications --- could be placed in opposition to cultural traditions whose origins lay elsewhere in the world [@mees_hitler_2004, p. 258]. Seeing Jewish participation in German cultural life not in terms of emancipation but as 'foreign domination' [@musiedlak_wilhelm_2021, p. 83-84], Marr thus employed a number of terms in order to emphasise what he saw as the alien nature of German Jews and their (in his view) imported culture. As well as _Judenthum_ or 'Judaism' (which, in German as in English, had the disadvantage of being the name of the Jewish religion), Marr sometimes described Jewish culture as 'Asianism' [@zimmermann_wilhelm_1986, p. 66-68]. However, he came to prefer the term 'Semitic', which brought Jews into his framework of linguistically-named races (as it was the name of the family to which the Jewish language of Hebrew belonged), and provided the derived word _Semitismus_, i.e. 'Semitism', to denote Jewish culture. In his hugely influential 1879 pamphlet, _The Victory of Judaism over Germanism: Viewed from a Nonreligious Point of View_, Marr argued that the secret conquest of Europe by Jews --- 'a tribe ... which ... was thoroughly hated by all the peoples of the Orient' [@marr_victory_1879_2009, p. 10] --- had begun during the reign of the Emperor Titus, who had (in bringing a small number of Jews to Rome after his razing of Jerusalem and the Second Temple) accidentally prompted a Jewish turn from military conflict with other Middle Eastern peoples to social corruption of Europeans [@marr_victory_1879_2009, p. 8-9], amongst whom those of the 'Germanic race' would prove particularly vulnerable:

> Germanism did not possess sufficient spiritual endurance to protect itself from Judaisation, and so Germany turned step by step into the new promised land for Semites ...

> This tribe, the Jews ... transformed all of its destructive potential into new forms and using these, vanquished the Occident, a victory it could not achieve in the Orient using fire and sword. 

> ...

> ... [O]ur outlook [today] is none other than a time when we Germans will live as slaves under the legal and political feudalism of Judaism. [@marr_victory_1879_2009, p. 14]

Although Marr was far from being the first writer to imagine the hidden hand of an international Jewish conspiracy behind every bad thing in the world, he was the first to transform this paranoid fantasy into a rallying cry for what many of his contemporaries would see as a justified 'war against the Jews' [@zimmermann_wilhelm_1986, p. 78]. Because 'Semitism' was by this point Marr's preferred word for Jewishness, he naturally used the word 'antisemitism' (or, in the original German, _Antisemitismus_) to describe the political programme that he subsequently laid out. That programme provided the much of the ideological foundation for National Socialism, or Nazism --- although some within that movement, including Hitler himself, prefered to replace 'Germanism' with fantasies of a supposedly prior 'Aryan' culture, hypothetical wellspring not only of everything Germanic, but also of the glories anciently achieved by Persia, Egypt, Greece, and Rome [@mees_hitler_2004, p. 268].

Marr was happy to identify as an antisemite, but many later adherents of his programme preferred to refer to their politics by alternative names. Indeed, the 1944 edition of Theodor Fritsch's _Handbook of the Jewish Question_ --- by that point, a standard Nazi text --- rejected the term 'antisemitism' as 'unsuitable, ... for the reason that there are other peoples with Semitic languages, such as the Arabs, who stand in complete opposition to the Jews' [Quoted in @zimmermann_wilhelm_1986, p. 113-115]. Nonetheless, the name stuck, and, moreover, broadened in meaning, such that it not only continued to denote the specific anti-Jewish politics of Marr and the Nazis, but also became the usual word by which to refer to _any_ form of anti-Jewish action, sentiment, or ideation --- even while the label is resisted by many of those involved.

The above discussion may strike the busy reader as unnecessarily deep in the historical weeds. However, there are two key points which could not have been made otherwise. The first is that it is enduring aspects of the ideological movement which emerged in response to the emancipation of Jewry that give contemporary Jew-hate its characteristic signature. The second is that the question of what to call that movement, and where to draw its boundaries, is far from clear. This is particularly important if sense is to be made of debates over the relationship between antizionism and antisemitism.

# Zionism, antizionism, and antisemitism
The above discussion has focused primarily on Europe. However, it has touched three times upon the Middle East: firstly in its mention of the terrible mistreatment to which Jews were historically subjected in much of the Muslim world, secondly in its inclusion of the Ottoman Empire in the narrative of Jewish emancipation through social progress, and thirdly in its allusion (via quotation) to continuing Arab hostility towards Jews which served as a basis of alliance with the Nazis. All of these things predated the foundation of the State of Israel.

Zionism --- the movement for national self-determination of the Jews in the historic homeland of the Jewish people --- began in the late 19th century, in response to the rise of political antisemitism (itself, as noted above, a backlash against emancipation). Its most effective spokesperson was Theodor Herzl, who in 1896 published a pamphlet under the title of _The Jewish State_. This work was primarily concerned with the practicalities of founding a state. However, it gave very clear expression to the rationale for doing so, i.e. the recognition that Jews would never be safe so long as long they had to rely on non-Jews for protection:

> No one can deny the gravity of the Jews' situation. Wherever they live in perceptible numbers, they are more or less persecuted. Their equality before the law, granted by statute, has become practically a dead letter. ...

> ... 

> Everything tends, in fact, to one and the same conclusion, which is clearly enunciated in that classic Berlin phrase: 'Juden raus!' (Out with the Jews!)

> I shall now put the Jewish Question in the curtest possible form: Are we to 'get out' now? And if so, to what place?

> Or, may we yet remain? And if so, how long?

> ... Were we as near to the hearts of princes as are their other subjects, even so they could not protect us. They would only feed popular hatred of Jews by showing us too much favour. By 'too much', I really mean less than is claimed as a right by every ordinary citizen, and by every tribe. [@herzl_jewish_1917_1896, 7-8]

Herzl's frame of reference was the situation faced by European Jews. But his proposals struck an immediate chord with the Jews of the Muslim world, whose situation was even worse than that of the Jews of Christendom. Gathering together in a single place offered many benefits for a dispersed and mistreated minority population, and, throughout the 19th century, there had been documented migrations of Middle Eastern Jews to the area around Jerusalem [@gilbert_ishmaels_2011, pp. 119–21]: a city in which they already constituted a majority (albeit a majority kept in an impoverished and vulnerable state by its Muslim rulers) [@gilbert_ishmaels_2011, pp. 106–7]. Indeed, there were Algerian Jews at the First Zionist Congress, and Zionist groups rapidly formed across the Middle East and North Africa [@gilbert_ishmaels_2011, pp. 135–39]. In the early 20th century, many thousands of Middle Eastern Jews escaped persecution and forced conversion by fleeing to the area now known as Israel [@gilbert_ishmaels_2011, p. 131, 147, 157-158].

Until the end of the First World War, that geographical area did not form a unitary territory, being divided between several administrative units. Following the break-up of the Ottoman Empire, it came to be administered by the United Kingdom, under a temporary arrangement known as the British Mandate for Palestine. Under British rule, Muhammad Amin al-Husseini --- the hereditary leader of the most powerful Arab clan in the area --- was appointed chief Islamic jurist, or Mufti, for Jerusalem. Seeking to guarantee his own continued authority in the post-Ottoman era, al-Husseini employed both religious and nationalist arguments against coexistence with the Jews, and allied himself both with the Nazis and with the Muslim Brotherhood [See @kuntzel_nazis_2024]. This was the world's first Islamist organisation. It was in many respects modelled on European Nazi and Fascist movements, and paralleled them not only in its ambition to restore the glories of a mythic past [@allington_islamist_2025, p. 35-36], but also in its hostility to Jews, seeking both to thwart Jewish aspirations towards soverignty and national self-determination, and to de-emancipate Jewry through the restoration of _dhimma_: the aforementioned discriminatory system under which Jews could only be second-class subjects of Muslim rulers [@allington_islamist_2025, p. 36-37].

> Early texts from the 1920s by Alfred Rosenberg --- later one of the central Nazi propagandists --- framed the 'Zionist menace' as an invasion of Palestine, decades before Patrick Wolfe's formulation of settler-colonial 'invasion'.  Rosenberg applied classical antisemitic notions of Jewish 'conquest', found in 19th century antisemites such as Wilhelm Marr and Eduard Drumont, equally to Jewish presence in Germany and to Jewish settlement in Palestine. [@louis-klein_antizionism_2026, n.p.]

In 1937, while on the run from the British authorities for his leading role in an uprising that killed 80 Jews, al-Husseini issued a pamphlet entitled _Judaism and Islam_, which was subsequently republished by the Nazis. It closely echoed Wilhelm Marr's fantasies of an ancient Jewish war against the European 'races' by accusing Jews of having secretly waged war on Islam for over a thousand years, and concluded with a call for retaliation and for racial as well as religious exclusivity in the lands then known as Mandatory Palestine:

> To my Muslim brothers of the whole world, I present the history and true experience that Jews cannot deny. The verses from the Quran and the hadiths prove to you that the Jews have been the most bitter foes of Islam and are still trying to destroy it. Do not believe them, they know only hypocrisy and guile.

> Keep together, fight for the Islamic idea, fight for your religion and existence! Do not rest until your land is free from the Jews!

> Do not tolerate the partition plan, for Palestine has been for centuries an Arab country and must remain so. [Translation published in @kuntzel_nazis_2024, p. 136]

Years of contributing to the Nazi war effort made al-Husseini's rhetoric still more strident, and in 1944, he issued the following battle cry:

> Arabs! _Rise as one and fight for your sacred rights. Kill the Jews wherever you find them. This pleases God, history and religion. This serves your honour. God is with you. [Translation published in @herf_nazi_2009, p. 213, emphasis in original]

Soviet antizionism.

All of the above threads coalesce in the Hamas:

> For a long time, the enemies have been planning, skillfully and with precision, for the achievement of what they have attained. ... With their money, they took control of the world media, news agencies, the press, publishing houses, broadcasting stations, and others. With their money, they stirred revolutions in various parts of the world with the purpose of achieving their interests and reaping the fruit therein. They were behind the French Revolution, the Communist revolution, and most of the revolutions we heard and hear about, here and there. With their money they formed secret societies ... for the purpose of sabotaging societies and achieving Zionist interests. ...

> You may speak as much as you want about regional and world wars. ... There is no war going on anywhere, without having their finger in it. [@hamas_covenant_1988, art. 22]



While the establishment of the state of Israel did not introduce a fundamental break into the historical development of antisemitism, it did ... especially in the Muslim world. ...

From treating Jews as a despised subordinate minority, dispersed throughout the world, to seeking to return them to their status as a subordinate minority, dispersed throughout the world ...

> the launch event for the 'Anti-Zionist Movement' ... featuring individuals such as David Miller, Rahmeh Aladwan, and Latifa Abouchakra, all of whom regularly post on social media about so-called 'Jewish supremacy' in control of the UK [@rosenberg_birmingham_2026]

'antizionism is a new form of Judeophobia only in the sense that what stands between the Jew haters and their enemy (the Jew) has changed', such that 'Israel is merely the new obstacle that stands between Judeophobes and the fulfilment of their unsubstantiated hatred against the Jews' [@fuchshuber_wilhelm_2019, p. 35].

It is my view that, while the existence of Israel has changed the material reality of the world in which Jews and antisemites live, it has not introduced a break in the historical development of antisemitism.

Hatred of Jews when they were dispersed throughout the diaspora is not discontinuous with hatred of Jews now that there is a nation in which they constitute the majority.

Indeed, it would require considerable mental gymnastics to argue that hating the Jews of Israel, and wanting Israel as a Jewish state to be wiped from the map, is not ...

> Classical antisemitism was anti-assimilationist: Jews were constructed as an alien minority incapable of belonging within European nation-states. Antizionism, by contrast, is structurally assimilationist: Jews may exist as individuals or as a religion, but Jewish nationhood and sovereignty are treated as illegitimate. Sovereignty becomes the offense. This inversion produces a systematic reclassification of the Jew—from infiltrating minority to colonizing majority—and generates a new set of cultural codings that are inverse to classical antisemitic constructions. [@louis-klein_antizionism_2026, n.p.]

Inverse Cultural Categories: Antisemitism and Antizionism

| Antisemitism | Antizionism |
| ------------ | ----------- |
| Rejection of Jews as minority | Rejection of Jewish majority-state' |
| Non-white, oriental, semitic | White settler, European |
| Jews as communists, Judeo-Bolsheviks | Jews as fascists, ultra-nationalists| 
| Tends to reject token Jews | Invests heavily in token Jews |
| Logics of elimination, physical genocide | Assimilation, cultural genocide |
| Uses biological sciences as authorisation | Uses social sciences as authorization' |
| Jews as a race | 'Zionism\' as an ideology |
| Jews infiltrate European states | Jews colonize non-European peoples |
| Exaggerates Jewish distinctiveness| Erases Jewish distinctiveness |

| Common logics |
| :-----------: |
| Jews as liars, manipulative, deceptive |
| Jews aligned with power and domination |

[@louis-klein_antizionism_2026, n.p.]

> In practice, nearly all definitions of antisemitism—IHRA, JDA, Nexus—presume that the task is to determine when antizionism 'crosses the line' into antisemitism. While this is often framed as distinguishing legitimate criticism from anti-Jewish bigotry, it effectively legitimizes antizionism by default as a form of criticism, recognizing only classical antisemitism as bigotry.

> The 'line crossing' is typically marked by echoes of historical antisemitism: media and government control, dual-loyalty accusations, or racial infiltration. Only when antizionism resembles these older patterns does it 'count' as bigotry. Meanwhile, the specific libels that define antizionism—colonizer, apartheid, genocide, white-coding—are cast as political or moral critique. 

> When the discourse turns to Israeli policy, or even Israel's right to exist, the framework shifts to concerns about 'double standards', or to Natan Sharansky's 'three D's' (delegitimization, demonization, and double standards).  Yet the core libels and symbolic structure of antizionism remain unnamed. There is a widespread assumption that 'antisemitic tropes' exist --- money, secret power, media control --- but not that there are distinctive antizionist tropes: white coloniser, Jewish supremacist, genocidaire. The same problems plague the category of 'political antisemitism', which does not specifically name antizionist libels but instead assumes that classical antisemitic tropes have simply been projected onto Israel. While such projection does sometimes occur, this framework fails to identify the specific ideological content of antizionist hate in the contemporary. In this way, the possibility that antizionism constitutes a distinct, contemporary formation of anti-Jewish bigotry is foreclosed in advance—by making antisemitism the generic category and classical antisemitism the normative model of hate. [@louis-klein_antizionism_2026, n.p.]

> Antizionism is ... not classical antisemitism projected onto Israel but a distinct ideological and symbolic complex whose core nucleus consists of the colonizer, apartheid, and genocide libels. Recognizing this requires moving beyond the 'line-crossing' frameworks that anchor the analysis in classical antisemitism. [@louis-klein_antizionism_2026, n.p.]

> The genocide accusation against Israel is precisely aimed at delegitimizing the state. It is to single it out as a state different from others. The ... determination of genocide ... is not based on the application [of] the strict legal doctrinal requirement of proving the intent to destroy. The attempt to argue that statements by Netanyahu, Gallant and Herzog amount to evidence of that intent involves taking the words out of context [and] ignoring that all three are talking about Hamas ... . It is particularly grotesque given the genocidal intent of the Islamic Republic of Iran and its proxies, that Israel is accused of the very crime that it is the victim of. [@strawson_israel_2026, n.p.]

> antizionists stress a radical discontinuity between the two. They argue that, even if traces remain, antisemitism ended in the mid-twentieth century, coinciding with the end of the Holocaust and the defeat of Nazism. This view is accompanied often by the belief that Jews are today, fully and finally, not only assimilated into their host countries, but also share the same privileges as the most dominant sectors of those societies. [@seymour_continuity_2019, p. 12]

> antizionism subsumes the antisemitism(s) of the past just as 'modern' or 'classic' antisemitism contained within it earlier, premodern forms of anti-Judaic praxis. In this way, any implication that non-antisemitic antizionism is legitimate can be challenged. I am arguing, in other words, that antizionism can never be legitimate contemporary political speech in the way that anti-Zionism may have been in the nineteenth century through to the mid-twentieth century. [@seymour_continuity_2019, p. 13]

>  that which the demonological Jew once was in older forms of antisemitism, demonological Israel now is in contemporary antisemitic anti-Zionism: uniquely malevolent, full of blood lust, all-controlling, the hidden hand, tricksy, always acting in bad faith, the obstacle to a better, purer, more spiritual world, uniquely deserving of punishment, and so on. [@johnson_written_2016, parag. 1.3]

> The necessity of recognising the existence of the ideology of antizionism as connected to, but distinct from, the ideology of antisemitism is more than a matter of semantics. The ideology of antizionism represents a new and novel 'reconfiguration' of the Jewish question. Although the nature of its anti-Jewish nature remains constant, its particular form and content is intimately connected to the specificities of Jewish emancipation. As much as Jewish emancipation adopts new forms so too does the ideological form of the Jewish question alter. In content and form, the ideology of antisemitism emerged and crystallized precisely at the moment Jews finally gained emancipation within the nation-state, achieved national self-determination. Moreover, antizionism, like antisemitism, was itself a combination of the old and the new, of continuity and discontinuity. Israel is new to the world and so too is the response from, and shape of, the Jewish question. The ideology of antizionism is that response. What is called for is not the subsumption of antizionism beneath antisemitism.
What is needed is recognition of the ways in which the ideology of antizionism has incorporated the ideology of antisemitism. [@seymour_continuity_2019, p. 22]

Community Security Trust in the UK:

> There were 1766 antisemitic incidents that showed explicitly anti-Zionist motivation, comprising 48% of the annual total, compared to 43% in 2024 and 31% in 2023. It is noteworthy that, over the last three years, the proportion of antisemitic incidents that display ideological anti-Zionism alongside anti-Jewish language or targeting has grown by a larger proportion than incidents that simply
contain Israel-related discourse. CST recorded 462 instances of anti-Jewish hate wherein the terms 'Zionism' or 'Zionist' were used, often as euphemisms for 'Jewishness' and 'Jew', or in conjunction with other antisemitic sentiment, rising from 423 cases in 2024 and 271 in 2023. Meanwhile, on 387 occasions, Israel, Israelis, or Jewish people were equated with Nazi Germany or
the Nazis: a rise from 333 such examples in 2024 and 257 in 2023. [@cst_antisemitic_2026, p. 5]

The most important thing to understand is that people who target Jews with hate crimes are not simply people in possession of negative stereotypical views of Jewish characteristics. Rather, they are people who have bought in --- to a greater or lesser extent --- to an ideology which purports to explain the history and present condition of the _entire world_, according Jews --- or 'Zionists' --- a central but villainous role in the story of the human race as a whole.

It is only through an appreciation of this ideoloogy that it is possible to make sense of many crimes which target Jews, and of the often heroic self-image of the perpetrators, who often see themselves not as lashing out against innocent and often defenceless individuals, but as striking a blow against an almost cosmic evil whose destruction is necessary for the redemption of the world as a whole. For example, an official Nazi Party publication declared in 1937 that 'Jewry's ruthless drive for world domination was stopped only by National Socialism' [Quoted in @bytwerk_believing_2015, p. 214], while just a few years later, Hasan al-Banna gave the following eulogy for Amin al-Husseini:

> The Mufti is Palestine and Palestine is the Mufti. Oh Amin! What a great, stubborn, terrific, wonderful man you are! All these years of exile did not affect your fighting spirit.

> Hitler's and Mussolini's defeat did not frighten you. Your hair did not turn grey of fright and you are still full of life and fight.

> ... Yes, this hero who challenged an empire and fought Zionism, with the help of Hitler and Germany. Germany and Hitler are gone, but Amin al-Husseini will continue the struggle.

> ... The armies of colonisation occupied Germany and hoped to catch Amin, but he was too clever for them. He managed to escape to France and now he returns to his people to resume the struggle against the criminal British and against Zionism. [quoted in @herf_nazi_2009, p. 244]

# X
The historically complex nature of contemporary Jew-hate can be seen from a brief consideration of recent crimes in Australia targeting Jews and locations associated with Jews. In 2024, the office of the then Attorney General, Mark Drefus was vandalised, with the words 'Who does AG Mark Dreyfus serve?' being painted on the pavement outside [@buckley_antisemitic_2025]. Although the _occasion_ of this attack was the Israel-Hamas war, it functioned by invoking the longstanding antisemitic trope of Jewish loyalty to supposed international Jewish interests (as seen in Marr's polemics, and also in more influential antisemitic works, such as the _Protocols of the Elders of Zion_). A few months later, the office of Josh Burns --- another Jewish politician --- was attacked by arsonists who spray-painted horns onto a photograph of Burns and added the slogan, 'Zionism is fascism' [@abc_activists_2024]. This act of vandalism combined pseudo-political discourse with 13th century European depictions of Jews as demonic horned figures [@bertman_antisemitic_2009]. Shortly afterwards, the Sydney suburb of Woollahra, known for its large Jewish minority, saw a number of arson attacks combined with anti-Israel graffiti [@buckley_antisemitic_2025, n.p.], while Mount Sinai College --- a Jewish school --- was vandalised not only with the message 'fuck the Jews' but also with the words 'killers' and 'merderers [sic]' [@buckley_antisemitic_2025, n.p.; @willan_heightened_2025, 00:15]: accusations which may have been occasioned by the Israel-Hamas war, but which date back to Medieval Europe.  

These examples (from which I have intentionally left out the massacre which prompted the official inquiry to which this report responds) illustrate two key points. The first is that contemporary acts of Jew-hate are saturated with historical baggage, and as such can only be understood by reference to the whole history of Jew-hate. The second is that, as at earlier stages in history, attacks on individual Jews are often rationalised in terms of the supposed evils of a Jewish or 'Zionist' collective. Whether we are to refer to this phenomenon as 'antisemitism' or as something else (and, for the sake of comprehensibility, this report will from this point onwards use the word 'antisemitism' in its ordinary sense, as defined by the Oxford English Dictionary at the beginning of this section), it is necessary to acknowledge these fundamental ideas.

This is, indeed, the only way in which it is possible to understand the statement issued by Iran-backed Islamist group Harakat Ashab al-Yamin al-Islamia on the occasion of the stabbing of two visibly Jewish men in London while this report was in preparation:

> Zionists were targeted by our lone wolves in the Golders Green area of London. The heroic act left two Zionists critically wounded and on the brink of death ...

> Historically, the Jews are the killers of Jesus Christ, and today, the Zionists are the killers of innocent women and children.

> We call upon all free people in the world, especially Muslims, to carry out qualitative operations in defense of humanity, occupied Palestine, al-Aqsa Mosque, and the prisoners. Furthermore, anyone who is able to kill Zionists should not hesitate. [Quoted in @starr_wounded_2026, n.p.]

How is it possible to identify knife attacks against random members of the British public --- apparently singled out, in one case, on the basis of his religious clothing, and, in the other, on the basis of his having emerged from a place of worship --- as 'qualitative operations in defense of humanity'? It is possible because this has been the logic of Jew-hate since the birth of ideological antisemitism in the late 19th century.

# Similarities and differences between antisemitism and other prejudices against racial, ethnic, and national groups
While any religious, ethnic, or national group may become the object of prejudice and undeserved hostility, antisemitism is unique in that it is founded on an ancient, but constantly evolving body of counter-factual belief concerning the world as a whole. The difference between antisemitism and other forms of group-based hate in this regard may be illustrated by example. Here, I have arbitrarily chosen the example of prejudice and hostility faced by Australians of Samoan origin.

Anti-Samoan bigotry is typically founded on a racist assumption that Samoans (and other indigenous Polynesians) are 'violent, unmotivated, and government-dependent' [@radclyffe_fob_2024, p. 1070]. This stereotype is very similar to stereotypes applied to many other immigrant groups, both in Australia and around the world. It would be extremely surprising to see anti-Samoan graffiti suggesting that Samoans are more loyal to Samoa than to Australia, or that Samoans are enemies of God, or that the Samoan government is fascist, or that the Samoan army kills non-Samoan children, or that Samoans and their supporters have an excessive influence on the Australian government --- and perhaps also the governments of other world powers. It would be still more surprising to see attacks on random Samoans justified as 'qualitative operations in defense of humanity'. The idea is bizarre, even verging on the ridiculous. Only Jews are spoken about in this way.

Where other minority groups are targeted, this is often on the basis of a barely-examined xenophobic tendency to see the majority community as superior. But where Jews are targeted, this is typically on the basis of a longstanding network of interlocking beliefs about world history and politics: beliefs which may play an analogous role to religious faith (as in the case of the atheist Wilhelm Marr) or which may in turn be deeply interlocked with religious faith (as in the case of the Christian and Muslim antisemites discussed above). Individuals and political movements are likely to go to much greater lengths in their attacks and persecutions if they see their target as irredeemably evil, supremely powerful, and a threat to the entire world, than if they are motivated simply by a small-minded dislike of people different from themselves. This is, ultimately, why Nazi Germany perpetrated the Holocaust, and why the Islamic Republic has forced the people of Iran into penury for the sake of an unwinnable proxy war against Israel.

None of this should be taken to imply that targeting of Jews is inherently _worse_ than the targeting of any other group: vandalism is vandalism, arson is arson, assault is assault, and murder is murder. What it means is that the targeting of Jews is motivated a peculiar way, and that this motivation is often very deeply ingrained, being strongly integrated with an individual's worldview, politics, and social identity. History shows us the extraordinary lengths to which Jew-haters have often been willing to go.

# Should governmental responses to antisemitism be based on a particular definition of antisemitic conduct?
Government responses to antisemitism should be based on an analysis of antisemitism. 

# The International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance Working Definition of Antisemitism

The International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance Working Definition of Antisemitism (henceforth, the IHRA Definition) defines antisemitism as 'a certain perception of Jews, which may be expressed as hatred toward Jews' [@ihra_definition_2016, p. 1].

The background to the IHRA definition.

> Antisemitic acts are criminal when they are so defined by law ...

> Criminal acts are antisemitic when the targets ... are selected because they are, or are perceived to be, Jewish or linked to Jews.

> Antisemitic discrimination is the denial to Jews of opportunities or services available to others, and is illegal in many countries. [@ihra_definition_2016, p. 2]

International adoption of the IHRA definition by governments and institutions.

Use of the IHRA definition to determine whether conduct is or is not antisemitic.

Controversies arising in relation to the IHRA Definition and its examples.

> Manifestations might include the targeting of the state of Israel, conceived as a Jewish collectivity. However, criticism of Israel similar to that levelled against any other country cannot be regarded as antisemitic. Antisemitism frequently charges Jews with conspiring to harm humanity, and it is often used to blame Jews for 'why things go wrong'. It is expressed in speech, writing, visual forms and action, and employs sinister stereotypes and negative character traits. [@ihra_definition_2016, p. 1]

Eleven specific examples, of which seven mention Israel:

> Accusing the Jews as a people, or Israel as a state, of inventing or exaggerating the Holocaust.

> Accusing Jewish citizens of being more loyal to Israel, or to the alleged priorities of Jews worldwide, than to the interests of their own nations.

> Denying the Jewish people their right to self-determination, e.g., by claiming that the existence of a State of Israel is a racist endeavor.

> Applying double standards by requiring of it a behavior not expected or demanded of any other democratic nation.

>  Using the symbols and images associated with classic antisemitism (e.g., claims of Jews killing Jesus or blood libel) to characterize Israel or Israelis.

> Drawing comparisons of contemporary Israeli policy to that of the Nazis.

> Holding Jews collectively responsible for actions of the state of Israel. [@ihra_definition_2016, p. 2]

# The main drivers of contemporary antisemitism in liberal democracies

Similarities and differences with regard to earlier periods in history.

Antisemitism 

Islamism and other Muslim supremacist movements.

# Antisemitism as a threat to Jews

# Antisemitism as a threat to social cohesion in liberal democracies
